casual-tea-asakusa.name: Casual Tea Ceremony (Tokyo Tourist Lounge Asakusa)
casual-tea-asakusa.editor_note: A relaxed, photos-welcome session — honest about being casual rather than formal. Platform-only operator with strong reviews; we haven't visited ourselves.

chazen-asakusa.name: Chazen Asakusa
chazen-asakusa.editor_note: A properly built tea room, complete with tsukubai water basin and nijiriguchi crawl-through entrance — rare among tourist venues. Handles larger groups well.
chazen-asakusa.price_note: ¥3,500 shared / ¥4,500 private session. Source: third-party articles, July 2026.

chazen-ginza.name: Chazen Ginza (main branch)
chazen-ginza.editor_note: A properly built tea room with tsukubai and nijiriguchi, next door to the Kabuki-za theatre — easy to make a full day of traditional culture.
chazen-ginza.price_note: ¥3,500 shared / ¥4,500 private session. Source: third-party articles, July 2026.

fukagawa-geisha-tea.name: Fukagawa Geisha Tea Ceremony
fukagawa-geisha-tea.editor_note: Reviews are weak, and the atmosphere is far from a formal tea gathering.

hamarikyu-nakajima.name: Hamarikyu Gardens — Nakajima-no-Ochaya
hamarikyu-nakajima.editor_note: This is a café, not a ceremony — which makes it perfect if a full tea ceremony feels like more commitment than you want.
hamarikyu-nakajima.notes: Matcha and wagashi in a garden teahouse; entry-level 'tea moment' rather than a ceremony.
hamarikyu-nakajima.price_note: Matcha + seasonal wagashi set 850 yen, plus garden entry 300 yen. Verify current status before publishing.

happoen-shirokanedai.name: Happo-en Garden Tea Ceremony
happoen-shirokanedai.editor_note: A genuinely beautiful garden venue, and the experience can be custom-designed to your group on request.

hisui-tokyo-ginza.name: HiSUi TOKYO (Ginza)
hisui-tokyo-ginza.editor_note: A multi-discipline cultural school; hosts take time to explain the seasonal meaning behind each utensil and the spirit of hospitality.

jidaiya-asakusa.name: Asakusa Jidaiya Tea Ceremony
jidaiya-asakusa.editor_note: Kimono and tea with chairs available, but the space is a studio rather than a tea room — enjoyable, though firmly an 'experience' rather than the real setting.
jidaiya-asakusa.price_note: Solo travelers pay ¥11,000; ¥5,500 pp for two or more, tax incl.

jugetsudo-ginza.name: Jugetsudo Tea Ceremony (Kabukiza Tower)
jugetsudo-ginza.editor_note: A very light, simplified matcha tasting rather than anything close to a ceremony.
jugetsudo-ginza.notes: Kengo Kuma-designed cafe; matcha starter set included as gift

koboji-temple-private.name: Private Tea Ceremony at Kobohji Temple (Mita)
koboji-temple-private.editor_note: The rare option to wear an Oshima Tsumugi silk haori is a lovely touch. Who actually performs the ceremony is unclear — not yet verified first-hand.
koboji-temple-private.notes: Venue correction by editor: temple is likely Kobohji (弘法寺) in Mita, not Asakusa. Verify address before publishing.

maikoya-asakusa.name: Maikoya Asakusa — Kimono Tea Ceremony
maikoya-asakusa.editor_note: Kimono dressing, private bookings, even nerikiri sweet-making add-ons — the pick if you want a broad cultural bundle rather than tea alone.
maikoya-asakusa.price_note: ¥6,300 with kimono including hair styling (women). Source: third-party articles, July 2026.

maikoya-shinjuku.name: Maikoya Shinjuku — Kimono Tea Ceremony
maikoya-shinjuku.editor_note: Same broad bundle as the Asakusa branch — kimono, private options, sweet-making — with the bonus of being walkable from Shinjuku Station.
maikoya-shinjuku.price_note: ¥6,300 with kimono including hair styling (women). Source: third-party articles, July 2026.

ocharu-nezu.name: Ocharu Tea Experience (near Tokyo University / Nezu)
ocharu-nezu.editor_note: A multi-tea tasting (black tea, gyokuro) rather than a tea ceremony. If matcha ritual is what you came for, look elsewhere — as a tea tasting it may suit you fine.
ocharu-nezu.notes: Modern multi-tea tasting (wakocha, gyokuro) rather than a formal ceremony.

shibuya-authentic-tea.name: Shibuya Authentic Tea Ceremony (50 min)
shibuya-authentic-tea.editor_note: Platform-only operator, not independently verified — book with expectations set accordingly.

shibuya-private-tea.name: Private Tea Ceremony Shibuya (shibuya-travel)
shibuya-private-tea.editor_note: Private 1-4 person format is a plus. Platform-only operator, not independently verified.

shizu-kokoro-asakusa.name: Shizu-Kokoro Tea Ceremony School
shizu-kokoro-asakusa.editor_note: The instructor has taught in the US and runs an actual tea school; this is the closest a visitor gets to a real keiko practice lesson.
shizu-kokoro-asakusa.notes: Koicha and usucha both served; guest and host roles; monthly seasonal themes. Priority for a first-hand review visit.

tea-room-kakoi-shibuya.name: Tea Room Kakoi (Shibuya)
tea-room-kakoi-shibuya.editor_note: Organic matcha tasting angle is appealing, but this is a platform-only operator we haven't verified first-hand.

urasenke-youwakai.name: Urasenke YOUWAKAI Tea Ceremony
urasenke-youwakai.editor_note: Well-known tea practitioners are involved in this school, and the tea room is unusually spacious. It takes a little courage to inquire, but this opens the door to the real depth of the culture.
urasenke-youwakai.notes: Usucha (thin tea) program. Hosted with involvement of Sosei Ajioka. Reservations accepted Mondays, Wednesdays, and Thursdays only. Same school as author — priority for first-hand review visit.
